figures.caption: Figure 4: Training and sequential rollout pipelines of RAE-NWM. During training, the generative network is optimized via a flow matching objective to predict the velocity field vθ matching the target velocity u(t). During inference, an ordinary differential equation (ODE) solver sequentially generates future states {ẑi+k, . . . } along a given action sequence {(ai→i+k, k), . . . } within a closed representation-space rollout loop. The frozen RAE decoder is applied exclusively for final pixel-level visualization.

Figure 10: Architectural designs for action and time-span encoding. The figure details the four injection variants compared in our ablation study: (a) Simple Addition, (b) MLP Fusion, (c) Scheduled Gate, and (d) our proposed adaptive Learned Gate.

Figure 1: Long-horizon generation comparison. We compare sequential rollouts from the baseline VAE-based NWM versus our DINO-based RAE-NWM. The VAE-based model exhibits severe structural degradation at later horizons (k = 12s, 16s), whereas RAE-NWM maintains strong structural integrity throughout the sequence.
Figure 2: Linear predictability of action-conditioned state transitions across various visual representation spaces, measured by the global R2 score over the prediction horizon step k.
Figure 15: Qualitative rollout results on the SCAND dataset.
Figure 14: Qualitative rollout results on the SACSoN dataset.
Figure 16: Qualitative rollout results on the RECON dataset.

Figure 5: Qualitative results of long-horizon generation. (a) Direct prediction at the 16-second horizon. NWM produces observations that completely deviate from the ground truth, whereas RAE-NWM maintains high geometric fidelity, demonstrating superior action-conditioned dynamics. (b) Sequential rollouts of RAE-NWM on the RECON and SCAND datasets, exhibiting strong structural consistency and spatial stability over extended horizons.

Figure 3: Architecture of the proposed RAE-NWM. Our model encodes context frames into a sequence of tokens zcond,i via a frozen DINOv2. The dynamics conditioning module then integrates the agent motion ai→i+k and prediction horizon k with the flow time t through a time-driven gating mechanism. Finally, the CDiT-DH backbone predicts the flow velocity vθ.
Figure 17: Qualitative rollout results on the Matterport3D dataset.
Figure 13: Quantitative performance on the SCAND dataset. Performance comparison between RAE-NWM and the baseline over a 16-second future horizon, generated iteratively step-by-step.

Figure 6: Long-horizon sequential rollout quality. Performance comparison between RAE-NWM and the baseline Navigation World Model on the SACSoN dataset over a 16-second future horizon, generated iteratively step-by-step.

Figure 11: Quantitative Analysis of the Time-Driven Dynamic Gating. The curves illustrate the temporal evolution of the dynamics conditioning strength across the continuous probability flow. Solid lines represent the median values, while the shaded regions denote the interquartile ranges (25th to 75th percentile) across diverse samples, highlighting the robustness of the learned modulation.
